title: Origami preliminary base
subtitle: Folding the preliminary base, the four-pointed symmetrical starting form for many origami models
excerpt: The preliminary base is a four-pointed symmetrical form that is the starting point for several classic origami models including the traditional crane and the waterbomb base variants. It requires eight folds from a square sheet and produces a form that opens into four flaps. Every fold in the sequence is a valley or mountain fold along an existing crease.
sourceNotes: The preliminary base is documented in Kunihiko Kasahara and Toshie Takahama, Origami for the Connoisseur (1987). The base itself predates modern origami documentation and appears in multiple mid-twentieth-century origami texts. No pre-1928 English-language source covers formal origami bases. Synthesised from contemporary origami documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A four-pointed origami base formed by combining a book fold and a diagonal fold sequence, producing an open square with four movable flaps.
A fold that brings the paper toward the folder, forming a V-shaped valley. Shown as a dashed line in diagrams.
A fold that pushes the paper away from the folder, forming an inverted V or mountain ridge. Shown as a dash-dot line in diagrams.

content.text: is sometimes called the square base. It is a prerequisite for the traditional crane, the lily, and several modular origami units. Once the fold sequence is memorised the base takes about 90 seconds to fold.

A square sheet of paper: standard origami paper (kami) is 15 × 15 cm and 60 to 70 gsm. Heavier paper is harder to fold to sharp points; lighter tissue-foil paper is better for complex models but too fiddly for learning the base.
Place the square coloured side down. Fold in half from bottom to top (a
Fold in half from left to right. Crease and unfold. The sheet now has a cross of valley-fold creases.
Turn the sheet over (coloured side up). Fold diagonally corner to corner (a
from the underside). Crease and unfold.
Fold on the second diagonal. Crease and unfold. The sheet now has four creases radiating from the centre.
Bring all four corners together toward the bottom, collapsing the sheet along all four creases simultaneously. Flatten to a small square with four equal flaps: two at the front and two at the back.

The finished preliminary base should be a square with four equal flaps that can be opened and rotated freely. All four points should be the same length. If one point is longer than the others, the original square was not folded exactly corner to corner. Unfold and re-crease, aligning corners exactly before collapsing.
